blog.title: Highlights from the First Half of 2026
blog.description:
My last personal post here was a New Year update over four months ago, though I did manage to share the books I read throughout the past year.

Over the past few years, I’ve hardly blogged here as much as I’d like. Whether it’s blogger’s block or just a lack of drive, work, or development (I can see you rolling your eyes), I’ve struggled to sit down and write a proper post.

Over the past few days, I’ve been trialling Wispr Flow (aff.), so I thought, “Why not put it to the test and dictate a blog post?”

I interspersed writing and dictating. So here is what happened.

Pune – from Christmas to New Year

India, or most specifically Pune, is just a three-hour flight from Dubai. That means heading down to Pune is a lot easier than it used to be when we lived in London.

In my New Year’s post, I mentioned that we had gone down to Pune for a few weeks over Christmas. I spent most of that time working on my plugins. After a long, relatively stressful year, I was looking forward to peace and quiet, which I thankfully got.

Amusingly, I didn’t release any plugins during this period, but I spent most of my time aligning the code across the various plugins to make migration between plugins much easier. I worked on Contextual Related Posts, Top 10, Better Search, and Knowledge Base Pro simultaneously. All of these have been released in the last several months, but I’ll get to that in a bit.

A quick stopover at Bangalore

We returned to Dubai in early January, right in time for my daughter’s school to resume. January was very busy at work, as usual. And it didn’t help that I picked up a nagging cough that wouldn’t go away.

We also made a relatively quick trip down to Bangalore in late January to visit Ashwina’s parents. We stayed at a Taj Hotel in Whitefield. I have to say it was shockingly expensive for a rather small room. I’m not sure whether it was because we booked late or because Bangalore somehow charges a fortune for fairly average hotels these days. We did thoroughly enjoy the food, though, which was definitely much better and more enjoyable over the few days we were there.

An uneventful February, almost

Although February was overflowing with work, coding, and everything else life could throw at me, it ended up being rather unremarkable. We finally felt that we were settling into our new life in Dubai. Between waiting months for our belongings to arrive from London, the Christmas break and the quick Bangalore trip, it had taken longer than expected.

With the media hinting at what might be coming, we limited our travel, and I had to shelve a few trips I’d pencilled in for early February.

My dad, en route back to Toronto, decided to stop over in Dubai for a week. We welcomed him with open arms, and it was brilliant for my daughter to have some proper time with her grandfather, rather than just seeing him on video calls. Dad has quite a bit of family and friends here in Dubai, so he ended up having a more active social life than we did. We did find time to head to our local Dubai Hills Mall for a bit of shopping and eating.

Watching my dad eagerly wake up each morning and head out for a walk around the area, I felt I was missing out. I decided to start walking. First, just around our community, and soon discovered a rather decent, long circular path near our place here in Dubai. Before long, I’d moved on from walking (I really haven’t the patience for it) to a mix of walking and jogging around the whole area.

That was, at least, until 28 February. The night before, I dropped my dad off at the airport for his early morning flight back to Toronto. I woke up early on 28 February – unfortunately, with a four-year-old, weekends or weekdays still have me waking up pretty early in the morning. I put on my trusty trainers and went for a jog around my newly discovered circular path. When I got back, I logged in and saw the news update that would change the world for months to come.

We all ended up working from home, and schools switched to remote learning. I was impressed by how quickly everyone got used to it, much like we did back during the pandemic.

The month in Pune

Unfortunately, many flights were cancelled, including our Emirates flight to Stansted. Since our trip to London was cancelled – and we’d paid quite a bit for those last-minute tickets – we decided to head to Pune again for the Eid break in March.

I had to get a few travel approvals given the situation, but the three of us managed to catch a late-night SpiceJet flight to Pune on 10 March.

Back in December, Ashwina and I had purchased the Pune flat, which meant a new place to call “home in India”. Whilst we didn’t initially plan to visit Pune, this unexpected trip allowed us to take care of some admin.

When we arrived in Pune, we found out that our electricity had been disconnected. Thanks to the heat and lack of power, everything in the fridge had gone off completely. Trust me, you don’t want to deal with that. It turned out there was an unpaid bill. Utility companies are quick to take your money up front, but not so patient when there’s a delay. We found that out the hard way – sorting out reconnection and getting everything set up again was a hassle. As for the fridge, it took a few days for the smell to dissipate. Luckily for me, Ashwina took care of most of this.

I worked for the first few days, then took the next two weeks off, which was the original plan. We also celebrated the little one’s birthday in Pune with a small family lunch at Tao-Fu. Since it was the middle of the week, the restaurant was quite empty. We let her pick the main dish, and we came away very impressed by her choice of the fried noodles. I enjoyed a few good cocktails as well. Highly recommended if you like Chinese food. Better than Mainland China across the road and around the same price. Good enough that we went back again the following week.

We also managed an ad hoc two-day trip to Mumbai to sort out a lot of bank work. As usual, we stayed at the ITC Hotel near the airport because we got ridiculously low pricing per night. It also allowed my mom (who lives in Mumbai) and my kid to spend some quality time together.

To Singapore and Hong Kong

One of the trips that I had shelved in February was to Singapore to meet with clients. The benefit of being outside the Middle East was that I could travel to Asia. So, in early April, I packed my suitcase and boarded an Air India flight from Pune to Delhi. It was my first trip on Air India after over 25 years. The last time was in 1998, and the experience was terrible. We were told our tickets weren’t confirmed, and upon our arrival in Muscat, we learned the airline had lost our suitcase. Fortunately, things have improved.

This was my first visit to Delhi airport. I definitely did not enjoy the transfer that required me to leave one terminal, walk to another, and then go through security all over again. That said, the Air India lounge at Delhi Airport was very good. I was able to get my customary G&T before boarding and ended up sleeping through most of the red-eye flight.

Although I had visited Hong Kong for a few days in early 2020, this was my first visit to Singapore, and I was looking forward to it. I do like to travel.

Having spent my whole financial career in the infrastructure sector, and with a bit of a thing for airports, I really do believe that an airport is the first proper impression a visitor gets of any city or country. A good airport experience sets the tone for the rest of the trip. Singapore’s Changi is famous for being one of those airports where you can actually enjoy spending time, at least on the airside. My arrival was smooth – off the plane and in a taxi in under half an hour. For an airport handling 70 million passengers a year, that’s impressive!

The hotel was about 30 minutes from the airport, and since I had landed early in the morning, I had enough time to check in and have a sumptuous breakfast there.

The next three days were a whirlwind of meetings around Singapore, but they did give me some time to properly catch up with Saurabh, who kindly took me around, as well as a few other friends.

The Singapore Skyline

Saurabh and I at the bar at the Mandarin Oriental in Singapore.

We left for Hong Kong late on Thursday, which meant I had a full day for meetings and time to catch up with the team there on Friday. Just as I was about to set off for the office, I realised I’d lost my office mobile. With the late-night travel and sheer exhaustion, I think it must have slipped out of my trouser pocket in the car. Unfortunately, we couldn’t get it back. On a more positive note, we took the team out for drinks, and I had sufficient time to spend with people in the HK office whom I had only met on Zoom.

I caught up with Saurabh for a coffee on Saturday morning before heading to the airport. Hong Kong Airport is a long way from the city centre, but the drive out there is worth it. The views of the ocean and the mountains are fantastic, especially in daylight. When I arrived in Hong Kong a few days earlier, it was dark, so I missed all of that.

The flight back to Delhi was quiet. I took Cathay for the return and had a short stop at Delhi Airport, just long enough to grab some dinner in the domestic lounge. After that, I caught my flight to Pune and landed early in the morning.

Back to Dubai

Normally, a simple return flight to Dubai shouldn’t make the headlines, but the adventures didn’t stop. Or maybe I should say that the adventures were just starting.

My return flight with SpiceJet was scheduled for Friday night from Pune airport.

The flight was delayed from the start because the incoming aircraft was late. We waited at the airport for over an hour before they finally boarded us onto the plane. Just as we were about to take off, maybe 10 minutes before, there was a problem with the runway. We were sent back to the terminal, and in the end, the flight was cancelled.

When an international flight gets cancelled, you have to go back through immigration, since you’re technically already out of the country. With a late-night cancellation at Pune airport, it’s even worse because most of the immigration staff have already gone home, so they all have to be called back in. That is a few hours of your life you don’t get back.

Instead of rebooking on SpiceJet from Pune, I decided to book an Emirates flight from Mumbai for late Sunday afternoon. This allowed me to spend time with my mom in Mumbai.

Back in Dubai

I reached Dubai late on Sunday evening. I walked into the house and caught a musty smell, which I assumed was because it had been locked up for a month. Until I went upstairs to the bedroom and found – wait for it – mould on the bed, the armchairs and more.

I rang Ashwina to fill her in, then booked a room at the Studio City Hotel, which is about 5 minutes away by car. I grabbed the same suitcases I’d brought from India, loaded them into the car, and drove across to the hotel. I ended up staying at the hotel for about 11 days.

Enjoying some pizza and sangria at the hotel restaurant

Over the next ten days, there was cleaning of AC vents, every piece of soft furnishing, a host of clothes and bedding, and pest control to restore the house to a livable state.

I also managed to damage my Patrol during all the trips between the hotel and my house. That’s when I found out, the hard way, the admin I needed to complete. If you’ve never dealt with this before, here’s a tip: if you’re at fault, call 999 straight away, even if you’re the only one involved.

I finally made it back to the office after 2 weeks in Dubai and squeezed in a quick trip to Qatar before Ashwina and our daughter returned home last week.

A Doha Syline

The past week was the school break because of Eid al-Adha, and schools start on Monday. That brings us back to the routine.

If you’ve made it this far and are a developer who uses WordPress for their product documentation, then buy Knowledge Base Pro.

webberzone.title: Knowledge Base REST API
webberzone.description: The WebberZone Knowledge Base plugin exposes selected functionality via the WordPress REST API. All endpoints live under the namespace and therefore share the base path: Developers can override route-level visibility without replacing the controller. The filter receives the route slug and the default public flag ( for read endpoints). Return values…

techtites.title: Optimise Images for Free: 5 WebP/AVIF Converters to make your Website faster
techtites.description:
Optimising images is a no-brainer if you care about website speed, SEO, or user experience. WebP, developed by Google, delivers superior compression without noticeably sacrificing quality — a win-win for performance and aesthetics.

The best part? All modern browsers now fully support WebP. You can also take it a step further with AVIF, the next-gen format offering even better compression — and yes, most of the tools below support that too.

Here are five excellent free online WebP converters that make switching to modern formats quick and painless.

1. TinyPNG

TinyPNG has been my trusty companion for image compression for a long time. Initially renowned for its PNG and JPEG optimisation, it’s now embraced WebP and AVIF formats. The interface is delightfully simple — drag and drop, select “Convert my images automatically”, and let it work its magic. For WordPress users, their plugin offers seamless integration, allowing the optimisation of up to 100 images monthly for free.

TinyPNG

TinyPNG WordPress plugin

2. Squoosh

Developed by Google, Squoosh stands out for its versatile features in image optimisation, including WebP conversion. Its real-time preview functionality lets users fine-tune compression settings, striking the perfect balance between image quality and file size. Squoosh’s simplicity and effectiveness cater to a wide range of users, offering an excellent platform for image optimisation.

3. BIRME

BIRME, an acronym for Batch Image Resizing Made Easy, excels in bulk image resizing and offers WebP conversion as part of its toolkit. This online tool streamlines the process for those dealing with extensive image libraries. BIRME’s user-friendly interface and batch-processing capabilities make it a practical choice for projects requiring efficient image conversion.

BIRME

4. Bulk Resize Photos

BulkResizePhotos is all about speed and privacy. Operating entirely in your browser, it ensures your images never leave your device. The tool excels in rapid batch conversions, offering options to resize, compress, and convert to WebP or AVIF. It’s my go-to when I need quick results without compromising on privacy.

Bulk Resize Photos

5. ShortPixel

ShortPixel’s online tool is a powerhouse for image optimisation. Beyond converting to WebP or AVIF, it offers intelligent compression that maintains image quality. The online tools allow you to drag and drop (or select) up to 50 files, and I have found this to have better compression than Tinypng generally. This has become my go-to image compression tool, replacing Tinypng.

Their plugin integrates seamlessly for WordPress users, automating the optimisation process and even serving images via their CDN for enhanced performance. It’s a comprehensive solution for those serious about site speed.

ShortPixel Online Tool

ShortPixel WordPress plugin

Final Thoughts

Each of these free online WebP converters has its strengths — whether you want granular control, lightning-fast bulk conversion, or just a smooth drag-and-drop experience. Adding them to your image workflow can drastically reduce page load times and improve Core Web Vitals.

 Want WordPress integration? TinyPNG and ShortPixel have free plugins. Need speed and privacy? BulkResizePhotos runs 100% in-browser. Like fiddling with settings? Squoosh gives you all the knobs and sliders.

No matter your needs, there’s a tool here that’ll save you time and bandwidth. Your users (and your PageSpeed score) will thank you.
